title_en: Introduction to Al-Muqaddimaat al-Mumahhidaat and Ibn Rushd the Elder
title_ar: مقدمة في المقدمات الممهدات وابن رشد الجد
content_en:
Al-Muqaddimaat al-Mumahhidaat (The Preparatory Introductions) is a major work of Maliki jurisprudence by Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn Rushd al-Qurtubi al-Andalusi (450–520 AH / 1058–1126 CE), known as Ibn Rushd the Elder or the Grandfather, to distinguish him from his famous philosopher-grandson Averroes (Ibn Rushd the Younger, 1126–1198 CE). Ibn Rushd the Elder was the leading Maliki scholar of his era in Muslim Spain (al-Andalus), serving as the chief judge (qadi al-jama'ah) of Cordoba and producing numerous important legal works.

Al-Muqaddimaat was written as an introductory commentary on the Mudawwanah — the great collection of Maliki jurisprudence compiled by Sahnun ibn Sa'id from the teachings of Abd ar-Rahman ibn al-Qasim, who was Imam Malik's primary student. The Mudawwanah is the foundational text of the Maliki school, and Ibn Rushd's Muqaddimaat serves to introduce each major section of the Mudawwanah with a systematic legal analysis that makes the work accessible to students approaching it for the first time.

The work is notable for its approach: rather than simply commenting on the Mudawwanah's rulings, Ibn Rushd provides introductory essays (muqaddimaat) that establish the general principles of each legal domain before the student encounters the specific rulings. This pedagogical innovation — introducing principles before details — reflects a sophisticated understanding of legal education and makes the Muqaddimaat one of the most intellectually sophisticated products of the Maliki tradition.

Ibn Rushd the Elder is also the author of Al-Bayan wat-Tahsil, an extensive commentary on the Mudawwanah, and Al-Muqaddimaat is sometimes understood as the introductory portion of this larger project. The two works together constitute Ibn Rushd's comprehensive engagement with the Maliki tradition's primary text.

Malik ibn Anas (93–179 AH), on whose teachings the Maliki school is built, established a legal tradition that was simultaneously text-based (grounded in the Quran, the Sunnah, and the practice of the Madinah community) and practically oriented (attentive to custom, public interest, and the prevention of harm). Al-Andalus, where Ibn Rushd worked, had a strong Maliki tradition that had been established by scholars returning from Madinah and developed by the independent scholarship of the Andalusian scholarly tradition.

The Maliki school in al-Andalus produced some of the most sophisticated legal and theological works in Islamic history. The intellectual environment of Cordoba and other Andalusian cities, where Islamic, Jewish, and Christian scholarly traditions interacted, stimulated legal scholars to develop systematic and comparative approaches to their subject. Ibn Rushd the Elder's Muqaddimaat reflects this intellectual environment — its careful organization and principled approach distinguishes it from the more discursive style of many other Maliki commentaries.

For students of Islamic jurisprudence, Al-Muqaddimaat provides an excellent introduction to the Maliki school's approach to major legal domains, written by one of the tradition's greatest minds. Its focus on foundational principles makes it relevant not just for Maliki students but for anyone seeking to understand how Islamic legal principles are derived and applied.
